title: The Silence of Joseph
subtitle: Joseph, finding Mary with child, resolves to send her away in secret — until the angel speaks

stillness: lest one sin through the rashness of haste.
Be still, then, and wait upon the Lord.
gospel: Now the birth of Christ came to pass in this way.
When his mother Mary was espoused to Joseph,
she was found to be with child.
But Joseph her husband, being a just man,
and not willing to expose her publicly,
was minded to put her away privately.
But while he thought on these things,
behold, the angel of the Lord appeared to him in his sleep,
fear not to take unto thee Mary thy wife;
for that which is born in her is of the Holy Spirit.
And she shall bring forth a son,
and thou shalt call his name Jesus;
for he shall save his people from their sins.
behold: Joseph her husband, weighing this matter, and ignorant of so great a secret of so great a mystery,
Yet, being just, he would not bring her out into the open — that is, divulge and defame her — lest she be stoned as an adulteress.
Or rather he would not lead her into his house to a continual dwelling together, because, on account of the power of the mystery he did not know, he reckoned himself unworthy of her company.
For after the conception her face so shone with divinity that, for its flashing, he could scarcely look upon her with steady eyes, and not without fear.
And so he wished to humble himself before so great a grace, reckoning himself unworthy of dwelling with so great a Virgin.
Nor did Mary pass through this without tribulation; for she weighed and saw Joseph troubled, and over this she herself was troubled not a little.
Yet humbly she kept silence, and hid the gift of God,
willing rather to be reckoned vile and wicked than to make known the gift itself.
See how great a tribulation and anxiety was upon them.
consider: Joseph believed more in her chastity than in her womb, and in grace more than in nature.
He saw the conception, and yet could not suspect fornication.
He held it more possible that a virgin could conceive without man, than that Mary could sin.
And see how the Lord permits his own to be vexed and tempted by tribulations, for their crowning;
but at the last the Lord provided for them both.
She prayed the Lord that he himself would deign to set a remedy, and take this tribulation from her and from her husband.
The angel was sent: that the just man should not, with a just purpose, do an unjust thing;
and that Joseph, understanding the cause of the conception, might bear himself more reverently with the Virgin.
Behold here a figure: by Joseph, doubting and then made sure through the angel, is signified the man wavering in faith or in morals,
who through a good preacher or confessor is made firm again.
From this we hold an argument: whatever we are admonished of by God, we ought to do at once.
prayer: Lord Jesus Christ, who art the impregnable wall of all who hope in thee,
be thou my refuge in tribulation;
behold my tribulations and my distresses, and have mercy on me,
and by all thy compassions come to my aid.
Behold my weakness, and with a father's mercy protect me,
that, upheld by thy providence, I may never be forsaken of thy consolation and mercy.
Remember, Lord, thy creature, and drive far from me the enemies that lie in wait for me;
that, fortified by thy mercy, I may know the sweetness of thy goodness in me,
and pay a worthy penance for my sins. Amen.

latinJewel.latin: Possibilius enim credebat virginem sine viro posse concipere, quam Mariam posse peccare.
latinJewel.english: For he held it more possible that a virgin could conceive without man, than that Mary could sin.
